inclusion_criteria: Human observational neuroimaging studies (e.g., cross-sectional, case-control, or baseline/pre-treatment data from longitudinal trials) comparing medication-free patients with major depressive disorder (DSM/ICD) to healthy controls
Cortical thickness quantified from structural MRI (T1-weighted) with extractable group-comparable data (e.g., means/SDs, statistics, or effect sizes)
MDD participants are drug-naive or adequately washed out at the time of MRI; if mixed medication status, separate data for the medication-free subgroup are reported
Peer-reviewed original research articles with full text
exclusion_criteria: MDD group using psychotropic medication at scan or unclear medication status/washout; mixed medicated/unmedicated samples without separate reporting for medication-free participants
No healthy control comparator or presence of major psychiatric (e.g., bipolar disorder, schizophrenia) or neurological comorbidities in the MDD group
Does not report cortical thickness from structural MRI (e.g., only cortical volume/surface area, DTI/fMRI/PET, or non-MRI modalities)
Non-human studies; reviews, meta-analyses, case reports, conference abstracts, or other non–peer-reviewed or duplicate publications
screening_criteria.description: Screening criteria to identify human observational studies comparing medication-free MDD patients with healthy controls that report cortical thickness derived from structural MRI.

included_articles.title: Regional increases of cortical thickness in untreated, first-episode major depressive disorder.
Increased prefrontal and parietal cortical thickness does not correlate with anhedonia in patients with untreated first-episode major depressive disorders.
Surface vulnerability of cerebral cortex to major depressive disorder.
Sex-specific alterations of cortical morphometry in treatment-naïve patients with major depressive disorder.
Surface-based analysis of early cortical gyrification and thickness alterations in treatment-Naïve, first-episode depressive patients during emerging adulthood.
Gray matter structural alterations in first-episode drug-naïve adolescents with major depressive disorder: a comprehensive morphological analysis study.
